title: Chicken dopiaza
subtitle: Onion-heavy chicken curry, with the onion added in two stages.
excerpt: Onion-heavy Anglo-Indian chicken curry built in two stages: a long-cooked onion-and-spice base for depth, then chunks of fresh onion added at the very end with a bite still in them. Sixty-five minutes of mostly hands-off cooking; rich, savoury, midweek-friendly. The double-onion method is the whole point of the dish.
sourceNotes:
Colonel A. R. Kenney-Herbert ('Wyvern'), Culinary Jottings for Madras (1878) — Project Gutenberg. Wyvern's writing on Anglo-Indian poultry braises records the spiced onion-base method that British-Indian restaurant cookery later formalised into the dopiaza: meat browned, an onion and spice base built separately, the two combined for a long covered simmer.

The name dopiaza (do = two, piaza = onion) is older than the restaurant version; the dish appears in Mughal-era cookery and travelled into British-Indian kitchens through Bangladeshi and Punjabi restaurateurs in the post-war years. Proportions here follow the curry-house tradition rather than a single citable text.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes for three months in a sealed container. The second-stage onion chunks soften on defrosting — they won't have the same bite as fresh — but the dish remains good. Defrost overnight in the fridge and reheat gently in a covered pan.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly in a wide sauté pan; triple-batches want a stockpot. The first-stage onion takes the same fifteen minutes regardless of batch size — patience here pays off in the finished sauce.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The whole dish can be made a day ahead and reheated; the flavour deepens overnight. If making ahead, hold the second-stage onion chunks until reheating and stir them in for the last five minutes of warming through.

recipeTools.notes: Wide 28 cm pan with a lid. The chicken browns and the sauce simmers in the same pan.
For turning the chicken thighs as they brown.

glossaryTerms.definition: Hindi-Urdu compound: do (two) + piaza (onion). A curry made with onion in two stages — long-cooked at the base for sweetness and depth, raw or briefly-cooked at the end for texture and bite. The technique predates the British curry-house version by centuries; Mughal cookery records it in the seventeenth century.

The slowly-cooked aromatic base of finely chopped onion (and sometimes carrot, celery, garlic) that begins many braised dishes across European and Indian-tradition cookery. Indian cooks call the equivalent stage bhuna or the onion masala; the principle is identical: cook the aromatics until they sweeten and concentrate before the rest of the dish goes in.

content.text: Chicken dopiaza is the onion-heavy chicken curry of the British-Indian restaurant tradition. The name says everything:
dopiaza

literally means "two onions", and the dish puts onion in twice. Sliced onion goes into the spice base early and cooks down to a sweet brown soffritto. A second round of onion, cut into rough chunks, joins for the last few minutes of cooking and stays with a bite still in it. The two textures together are the whole point of the dish.
The method is straightforward. Brown the chicken thighs in a wide pan; lift them out. Build the spice base in the same pan:
soffritto

of finely chopped onion, then ginger-garlic paste, then ground spices bloomed in fat, then tomato. The chicken goes back in for a covered simmer. The second onion joins at the end, off the heat just long enough to take the raw edge off.

Two notes worth flagging. First, boneless thigh is the right cut: it stays juicy through a forty-minute simmer where breast turns to leather. Second, the second-stage onion wants to be cut into 2 cm chunks, not finely chopped — large enough to keep their shape and add texture in the finished dish. A fine dice will dissolve into the sauce and the dopiaza will read as a single-onion curry.

attrs.title: Why two stages of onion
attrs.body: Onion does two different jobs in this curry. Slow-cooked, finely chopped onion sweetens and concentrates: it adds the deep brown background note that the spice base sits in. Raw or briefly-cooked onion chunks add a sharper, fresher note and a bite to the finished texture. Combining the two gives the dish a depth and a brightness at the same time. A single-onion version (either fully cooked or fully raw) lacks one of those notes; the double-onion method is the dish's signature.

content.text: What you need

items.prepNote: cut into 2 cm chunks, for the finish
a small handful, chopped, to finish

content.text: A wide sauté pan with a lid handles the whole dish; a smaller pan crowds the chicken and means it steams rather than browns. A microplane grates the ginger and garlic in under a minute.
Method
Brown the chicken

Heat {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a wide pan over a medium-high heat. Season {{chicken-thighs-boneless}} chicken thighs with a pinch of salt. Lay them in the pan in a single layer; don't crowd them. Brown for three minutes a side until each piece has a good colour. Lift onto a plate and set aside. They won't be cooked through yet, which is fine.
Cook the first-stage onion

Lower the heat to medium. Add {{cumin-seeds}} of cumin seeds to the pan and let them sizzle for fifteen seconds. Tip in {{onion}} finely chopped onions (the first-stage onions) along with a pinch of salt. Cook for fifteen minutes, stirring every couple of minutes, until the onion is deeply golden and starting to turn jammy at the edges. This is the soffritto stage; rushing it costs the dish flavour.
Bloom the spices

Stir in {{ginger-root}} of grated ginger, {{garlic}} of grated garlic, and {{chilli-red}} chopped red chilli if using. Cook for a minute until fragrant. Add {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée, {{cumin-ground}} of ground cumin, {{coriander-ground}} of ground coriander, {{turmeric}} of turmeric, and {{chilli-powder}} of chilli powder. Cook for two minutes, stirring constantly, until the oil starts to pull away from the paste at the edges of the pan. The smell should turn toasty rather than sharp.
Simmer the sauce

Tip in {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes, crushing them against the side of the pan with the spoon. Add {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and 200 ml of water. Bring to a gentle simmer. Return the chicken thighs to the pan with any juices that have pooled on the plate. Cover, lower the heat to low, and cook for twenty-five minutes. The sauce should reduce by a third and the chicken should be fork-tender.
Add the second-stage onion

Lift the lid. Stir in the second-stage onion chunks. Cover again and cook for five minutes; the chunks should warm through and soften slightly but keep their shape and a little crunch. This is the difference between dopiaza and a generic chicken curry.
Finish

Stir in {{garam-masala}} of garam masala. Taste and adjust the salt. Scatter with chopped fresh coriander. Serve with basmati rice or naan, and a side of pilau or a fresh tomato-and-onion salad if you want a fuller plate.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Dopiaza goes wrong at three points: under-coloured first-stage onion, a sauce that tastes raw or thin, and second-stage onion that's gone soft instead of holding its bite.

items.cause: First-stage onion not cooked long enough.
items.fix: The finely chopped onion needs the full fifteen minutes to turn deep gold and jammy; this is where the sauce gets its depth. If the onion is still pale-yellow at ten minutes, give it another five before the spices go in. The visual cue is the onion starting to look slightly translucent and brown at the edges.

items.symptom: Sauce harsh, with a raw-spice edge.
items.cause: Spices not bloomed before the tomato went in.
items.fix: The ground spices need two minutes in the hot fat with the onion paste before the tomato joins. The visual cue is the oil starting to pull away at the edges of the spice paste; the smell turns toasty. Splash in a tablespoon of water if it threatens to stick.

items.symptom: Second-stage onion has dissolved into the sauce.
items.cause: Chunks too small, or cooked too long after adding.
items.fix: Cut the second-stage onion into 2 cm chunks at minimum — bigger is fine. Add only at the very end, for five minutes covered; longer than that and the chunks soften past the point of useful texture. The whole point of dopiaza is two onion textures in the finished dish.

items.cause: Breast used instead of thigh, or simmered too long.
items.fix: Boneless thigh stays juicy through the twenty-five minute simmer. If you must use breast, drop the simmer time to fifteen minutes and add the breast in larger pieces. Twenty-five minutes is the maximum for boneless thigh; beyond that the meat starts to dry out.

items.cause: Tinned tomatoes were acidic, or not enough cooked onion to balance them.
items.fix: Some tins of tomatoes are sharper than others. A pinch of sugar at the simmering stage rounds the acidity off, or use half tinned tomatoes and half fresh chopped tomato. The deep-golden first-stage onion adds sweetness that balances the tomato; if the dish is sharp, the onion was probably under-cooked.

content.text: Variations

Lamb dopiaza uses boneless lamb shoulder cut into 3 cm chunks, browned the same way, and simmered for sixty to seventy minutes instead of twenty-five. King prawn dopiaza is much faster: skip the browning stage, add the raw prawns to the sauce in the last five minutes alongside the second-stage onion. Vegetarian dopiaza swaps the chicken for cauliflower florets and chickpeas — adjust the simmer to fifteen minutes for the cauliflower. A small handful of dried fenugreek leaves crumbled in with the garam masala adds a richer, curry-house finish.
Make ahead, freezing, leftovers

The whole dish can be made a day ahead and reheated; the flavour deepens overnight. If making ahead, hold the second-stage onion chunks until reheating and stir them in for the last five minutes of warming through so they keep their bite. The finished curry freezes for three months in a sealed container; defrost overnight in the fridge and reheat covered. Cold leftovers reheat well in a low oven or a covered pan with a splash of water.
Where this dish lives

Dopiaza is one of the older names in the British curry-house canon. The word and the technique go back to Mughal-era cookery in the seventeenth century, where the two-onion method appears in court manuscripts as a way of building a sauce with both depth and brightness. It travelled into British kitchens through Bangladeshi and Punjabi restaurateurs in the post-war years, becoming a fixture on curry-house menus by the 1970s.

At a British family table it sits comfortably alongside tikka masala, korma, and jalfrezi as one of the standard medium-spice curries — though the dopiaza's signature is texture rather than heat. The double-onion grammar is also a useful technique to learn beyond this single dish: lamb dopiaza, prawn dopiaza, and the various regional vegetarian versions all rest on the same principle. Once a cook has the timing of the two onion stages right, the rest is a question of swapping the protein and the heat.
